title: Designing a handwritten recipe collection journal
subtitle: Laying out a consistent recipe page format that works for every dish type
excerpt: A handwritten recipe journal is a functional document and a personal record. A consistent page format that handles ingredients, method, timing, and notes in a set place makes the journal useful to cook from as well as to browse. The format should accommodate both a three-ingredient dressing and a multi-stage cake without redesign.
sourceNotes: Common journalling practice for recipe collection. No single primary source.

glossaryTerms.definition: A protruding label at the edge of a page that identifies its contents. In a recipe journal, index tabs cut into the page edges (or made from sticky labels) allow the journal to open directly to a section without searching through every page.

The title area at the top of a page or spread that names the content. In a recipe journal, the masthead holds the recipe name, the serving count, and any identifying notes such as source attribution or dietary information.

content.text: A recipe journal is used in the kitchen, often while cooking and often with wet hands. The layout must be legible at a distance. Ingredient quantities and temperatures must be easy to find at a glance. The page must survive the odd splash.

You also need a pencil for layout planning, a fine-liner or pen for headings, a regular pen for ingredient lists, and a ruler. If the journal will be used daily in the kitchen, laminate the finished pages with sticky-back laminate.

block at the top of the page, 3 to 4 cm deep. Write the recipe name in your largest clear hand, followed by a smaller serving count and key details: prep time, cook time, temperature. These are the facts you need most urgently when using the recipe.

Below the masthead, set up two columns. A narrow left column (one third of the page width) holds ingredients and quantities. A wider right column holds the method steps. This mirrors how most cookbooks are laid out and makes quantities easy to scan.

In the ingredients column, list each ingredient on its own line. Write quantity first (in bold or a different size), then ingredient name. Keep the unit consistent: grams rather than a mix of 'a handful', '200g', and 'two of'. Consistency makes the journal easier to scale or adapt later.

In the method column, write the steps as short numbered instructions. Use a separate line for each step. Long recipes that overflow a single page continue on the reverse: mark 'continued overleaf' at the bottom of the recto and 'continued' at the top of the verso.
Leave a notes section at the bottom of the page, a narrow band 2 to 3 cm deep, below a rule. Record variations, results, and adjustments here. This is the working record of the recipe over time.
A recipe journal becomes much more useful with

that identify sections (baking, soups, salads, preserves, etc.). Cut a small notch from the outer edge of the first page in each section and reinforce with a paper label. Alternatively, use purchased section dividers or create a separate index page at the front of the journal.
